description: Velocity scaling factor applied when the robot enters a SLOWDOWN polygon zone. The incoming velocity command is multiplied by this ratio. Only used when action_type is 'slowdown'.
impact: Lower value slows the robot more aggressively near obstacles. A value of 1.0 effectively disables the slowdown. Tune alongside zone size to achieve the desired approach behaviour.

description: Topic providing the robot footprint geometry used for APPROACH polygon collision prediction. The footprint is projected along the simulated trajectory to check for intersections with the zone. Only used when action_type is 'approach'.
impact: Must match the footprint topic published by the costmap (or any other publisher). An incorrect topic causes the approach calculation to use a zero or stale footprint, leading to incorrect collision predictions.

description: List of directories to search for Behavior Tree XML files. At startup each directory is scanned for .xml files so they can be referenced by filename in the default_nav_to_pose_bt_xml / default_nav_through_poses_bt_xml parameters or passed as a goal.
impact: Add custom BT directories here to make your own trees available without specifying full paths. The package-share path is always searched by default.

description: Maximum time the BT action server will wait for a cancel request to be acknowledged and the tree to reach a terminal state before returning CANCELED.
impact: Too small a value may cause the navigator to report failure on cancel before the BT has finished cleaning up. Increase if plugins need extra time to abort mid-action.

description: List of action-name prefixes used to harvest error codes from the BT blackboard after execution. Each prefix is combined with '_error_code' to form the blackboard key read at the end of a BT tick.
impact: Must include a prefix for every action server whose error codes should propagate to the navigator result. Missing entries cause error codes to be silently dropped and result in unhelpful UNKNOWN failure reasons.

description: Time window for the OdomSmoother used to compute the robot's current speed from the odometry topic. The smoother averages odometry messages received within this window.
impact: Larger values produce a smoother speed estimate but add lag, which affects estimated-time-to-goal feedback accuracy. 0.3 s is a good default for typical odometry rates.

description: TCP port used by the Groot2 monitoring server for the navigator. Declared per-navigator sub-namespace (e.g. navigate_to_pose.groot_server_port). Both navigate_to_pose and navigate_through_poses default to 1669.
impact: Only relevant when enable_groot_monitoring is true. Change if the port conflicts with another service. Both navigators share the same default so they must be given different ports when run simultaneously.

description: Controls ROS 2 service-call introspection for the navigator's action server. Valid values: 'disabled' (off), 'metadata' (record request/response metadata only), 'contents' (record full request/response data). Requires rclcpp >= 29.
impact: Enabling introspection is useful for debugging action call flow but adds overhead. Use 'metadata' for lightweight tracing and 'contents' only during focused debugging sessions.

description: Maximum path length (meters) searched forward along the global path from the last known closest segment when computing distance-to-goal feedback. Declared per-navigator sub-namespace (e.g. navigate_to_pose.search_window).
impact: A larger window ensures the closest point is found even after large odometry jumps, at slightly higher CPU cost. Too small a value may lock onto a stale segment, causing incorrect distance-remaining estimates.

description: Time step used when propagating sampled trajectories in the MPPI optimizer. Must equal the controller period (1/controller_frequency); MPPI will raise an error if they differ.
impact: Shorter steps improve trajectory accuracy but increase per-step computation. A value that does not match the controller period will cause MPPI to refuse to start.

description: Number of time steps in each sampled trajectory. Horizon length = time_steps * model_dt (e.g. 56 * 0.05 s = 2.8 s look-ahead).
impact: More steps extend the planning horizon, improving anticipation of distant obstacles and curves at the cost of higher memory and compute per control cycle. Too few steps causes the optimizer to react too late.

description: Number of random trajectory samples evaluated per optimization step. The nav2_bringup default YAML uses 2000.
impact: Larger batches improve solution quality and robustness in cluttered environments at the cost of proportionally higher CPU/GPU load. Reduce on resource-constrained hardware.

description: Number of MPPI optimization iterations performed per control cycle. Each iteration re-samples noise around the previous best control sequence.
impact: More iterations refine the control solution and can improve tracking in tight spaces, but multiply computation time proportionally. 1 is sufficient for most environments.

description: Softmax temperature lambda used in the MPPI importance-sampling weight: w_k = exp(-cost_k / temperature). Controls how sharply the optimizer focuses on low-cost trajectories.
impact: Lower values make the controller more aggressive by concentrating weight on the very best trajectories; higher values average over more samples, giving smoother but potentially sub-optimal commands.

description: Discount factor applied to the control-effort regularization term in the MPPI cost. Scales how much deviation of sampled controls from the nominal sequence is penalized.
impact: Increasing gamma penalizes control sequences that deviate strongly from the previous solution, producing smoother command trajectories. Too high a value can restrict the optimizer from finding better paths.

description: Pluggable kinematic motion model used to propagate sampled trajectories. "diff_drive" (non-holonomic), "omni" (holonomic with independent vy), or "ackermann" (car-like with minimum turning radius).
impact: Must match the robot drive type. A mismatch causes trajectories to be kinematically infeasible, degrading path tracking. Requires a matching motion model plugin to be loaded.

description: Maximum forward (positive-x) velocity allowed for sampled MPPI trajectories. Acts as a hard kinematic constraint: any trajectory sample that exceeds this value is penalized by the ConstraintCritic.
impact: Increase to allow faster navigation on open terrain. Decrease to enforce conservative speed in tight or crowded spaces. Must be consistent with the robot's physical velocity limits; setting it above the hardware limit has no practical effect.

description: Maximum backward (negative-x) velocity allowed for sampled MPPI trajectories. Must be negative or zero. The optimizer corrects a positive value to its negative automatically with a warning.
impact: Setting closer to zero prevents reversing; a more negative value allows the robot to back up further when recovering from blocked paths. If reversing is undesirable, set to 0.0.

description: Maximum angular velocity magnitude allowed for sampled MPPI trajectories. Applies to both clockwise and counter-clockwise rotation.
impact: Increase to allow sharper in-place turns; decrease to produce smoother, wider arcs. Should match the robot's angular velocity hardware limit.

description: Maximum forward acceleration limit applied during MPPI trajectory propagation. The optimizer takes the absolute value of this parameter, so negative inputs are accepted.
impact: Lower values produce smoother acceleration profiles and reduce actuator wear but may slow response to new goals. Higher values allow faster speed changes at the cost of jerkier motion.

description: Maximum deceleration limit (negative value) applied during MPPI trajectory propagation. Must be negative or zero; the optimizer negates positive values automatically with a warning.
impact: A value closer to zero (e.g. -1.0) limits braking harshness, useful on slippery surfaces. A more negative value (e.g. -5.0) allows emergency stops but may cause wheel slip.

description: Standard deviation of the Gaussian noise added to the forward-velocity control dimension when generating MPPI trajectory samples. Controls exploration width in the vx direction.
impact: Larger values increase trajectory diversity, helping MPPI escape local minima, but may produce physically infeasible samples and raise cost variance. Smaller values make sampling conservative, useful when the robot must stay on a tight corridor.

description: Standard deviation of the Gaussian noise added to the angular-velocity control dimension when generating MPPI trajectory samples. Controls turning exploration breadth.
impact: Higher values allow wider turn exploration, benefiting navigation around tight corners, but can create erratic steering. Lower values produce smoother but potentially tunnel-visioned trajectories in highly curved paths.

description: Maximum lateral (y-axis) velocity for holonomic robots. Not used by differential-drive or Ackermann motion models — only active when motion_model is 'omni' or 'diff_omni'.
impact: Raising this allows faster sideways movement on holonomic platforms, reducing time to re-align. On diff-drive robots this value is unused; setting it has no effect.

description: Standard deviation of Gaussian noise added to the lateral-velocity control dimension when generating MPPI trajectory samples. Only meaningful for holonomic motion models.
impact: Higher values produce broader lateral exploration in sampled trajectories, helping holonomic robots navigate around side obstacles. Irrelevant for diff-drive robots.

description: Maximum positive lateral (y-axis) acceleration constraint applied during MPPI trajectory propagation. Used only with holonomic motion models.
impact: Increasing allows faster lateral direction changes on holonomic robots. Too high a value can cause physically unrealisable trajectories if the hardware cannot achieve it.

description: Maximum negative lateral (y-axis) acceleration constraint applied during MPPI trajectory propagation. Must be negative (or zero). If set positive, MPPI will flip the sign and log a warning.
impact: Symmetric with ay_max; controls how quickly the robot can decelerate or reverse lateral motion. Relevant only for holonomic platforms.

description: Maximum angular acceleration constraint applied when propagating MPPI trajectory samples. Limits how fast the yaw rate can change between consecutive time steps.
impact: Lower values produce smoother, more physically realisable yaw profiles. Higher values allow sharper turns in sampled trajectories but may exceed hardware limits, causing the robot to track sub-optimally.

description: When true, MPPI propagates trajectories in open loop, ignoring the robot's current odometry velocity as the initial state. The previous optimal control sequence is used as the starting point instead.
impact: Useful when odometry is unreliable or noisy. Open-loop mode can reduce jitter caused by odometry spikes but makes the controller less reactive to sudden velocity changes. Leave false for robots with good odometry.

description: When true, publishes the optimal (winning) MPPI trajectory as a nav_msgs/Path topic for visualisation in RViz.
impact: Enables real-time debugging of the selected trajectory. Negligible CPU cost; disable in production if the topic is not consumed to avoid unnecessary serialization.

description: When true, fresh Gaussian noise samples are drawn from scratch every control cycle. When false (default), the previous noise sequence is shifted and only the new tail sample is generated, saving compute.
impact: Setting to true provides statistically independent samples each cycle which can escape local minima in adversarial environments, at the cost of higher random-number-generation overhead. The default (false) is preferred for real-time performance.

description: Penalty added to a sampled trajectory's total cost when any of its evaluated points is found to be in full collision (LETHAL_OBSTACLE). The enormous value ensures that fully-colliding trajectories are never selected.
impact: Increasing this value beyond the default rarely changes behaviour since collisions are already ranked last. Lowering it risks selecting colliding trajectories; do not lower below the order-of-magnitude of other critic weights.

description: Flat penalty added per trajectory point whose costmap value is at or above near_collision_cost (i.e. within the inscribed-inflation band). Acts as a hard repulsive region just outside lethal space.
impact: Higher values make the robot avoid the near-collision band more aggressively. Too high can prevent passage through narrow doorways; too low allows the robot to linger near obstacles.

description: Costmap cell value threshold at or above which the critical_cost penalty is applied rather than the normal repulsive weight. Default of 253 corresponds to INSCRIBED_INFLATED_OBSTACLE — the ring immediately inside lethal space.
impact: Lowering this threshold widens the near-collision zone so critical_cost is applied at greater distances from obstacles, producing a larger safety buffer. Setting it higher than 253 triggers a warning and has no practical effect.

description: When the remaining local path length falls below this value the CostCritic disables its obstacle-repulsion term. Goals near walls would otherwise accumulate large repulsive costs, preventing the robot from reaching them.
impact: Increase if the robot stops short of goals placed close to obstacles. Decrease if you observe the robot cutting corners near the final approach. Set to 0 to always apply repulsion.

description: Stride used when iterating over trajectory time-steps inside CostCritic: only every Nth point is evaluated against the costmap. Reduces the number of costmap lookups per trajectory.
impact: Larger strides speed up the critic at the risk of missing narrow obstacle crossings between sampled points. A step of 1 evaluates every point (maximum safety); 2-4 is a common trade-off for real-time performance.

description: Remaining local path length below which GoalCritic activates and pulls sampled trajectories toward the goal position. Above this threshold the path-following critics handle alignment.
impact: Increasing the threshold causes GoalCritic to engage earlier (farther from the goal), which can improve final approach accuracy but may conflict with path-following critics over a larger distance. Decrease if the robot deviates from the path too early near the goal.

description: Time horizon over which the TrajectoryValidator checks the selected optimal trajectory for collisions before it is sent to the robot. Converted to a number of trajectory samples via collision_lookahead_time / model_dt. Capped at time_steps if larger.
impact: Increasing this value catches collisions that appear later in the trajectory at the cost of slightly more costmap lookups per cycle. Decreasing it speeds up validation but may let the robot commit to a trajectory that enters collision shortly after the horizon.

description: Maximum time to wait for a costmap update before the current control cycle proceeds. Set to 0 or negative to disable waiting.
impact: Increase if the costmap is slow to update and you see stale-costmap warnings. Too high a value stalls the control loop; 0 disables the wait entirely.

description: Request soft real-time scheduling priority for the controller thread. Requires the process to have the appropriate OS capabilities (e.g. CAP_SYS_NICE).
impact: Enabling reduces control-loop jitter on loaded systems, which can improve trajectory tracking. Has no effect if the process lacks the required privilege.

description: List of progress checker plugin names. Each name must resolve to a plugin type via its '.plugin' sub-parameter.
impact: Requires node restart. The progress checker monitors whether the robot is making progress; failure triggers a recovery or error. Swapping the plugin changes how progress is measured.

description: List of path handler plugin names responsible for pre-processing the incoming path before passing it to the controller.
impact: Requires node restart. The default FeasiblePathHandler prunes and validates the path. Changing this affects how path feasibility and pruning are handled.

description: Duration of the odometry history window used by OdomSmoother to compute a moving-average of the robot's current velocity.
impact: A longer window gives smoother velocity estimates but increases latency. A shorter window is more responsive but noisier. Tune based on odometry quality.

description: Maximum path length (in meters) to search ahead from the robot's current position when finding the closest point on the path.
impact: A larger window prevents the robot from snapping back to a distant segment but costs more CPU per cycle. Reduce on long paths with tight compute budgets.

description: Maximum path length ahead of the robot (from the closest point on the path) to include in the transformed local plan passed to the controller. Effectively a forward look-ahead window along the path.
impact: Increase to give the controller a longer segment for smooth curve handling; decrease to limit the path horizon and reduce unnecessary computation on very long paths.

description: If true, throws an InvalidPath exception when the global plan contains only a single pose (a "unit path"). Prevents the controller from attempting to track a degenerate one-point plan.
impact: Enable to make the system fail-fast if the planner returns a trivial path; leave disabled (default) to let the controller handle single-point plans gracefully.

description: When true, the path handler detects direction-reversal (inversion) points in the global plan and restricts the controller to only the segment up to the first inversion. Once the robot reaches the inversion within tolerance, the handler advances to the next segment.
impact: Required for paths that include backward driving segments. Without this, the controller may try to drive through an inversion point instead of stopping and reversing.

description: When true, the path handler inserts mandatory in-place rotation constraints wherever the path heading changes by more than minimum_rotation_angle. The controller must complete the rotation before advancing to the next segment.
impact: Useful for differential-drive robots navigating tight turns. Requires minimum_rotation_angle to be set appropriately. Has no effect if minimum_rotation_angle is 0.

description: Euclidean distance tolerance used to decide whether the robot has reached a path inversion (or rotation) constraint point. Once the robot is within this distance AND within inversion_yaw_tolerance, the handler advances to the next path segment.
impact: Smaller values require a more precise stop at the inversion point before the next segment is released; larger values allow the robot to advance sooner but may cause overshooting.

description: Angular (yaw) tolerance used together with inversion_xy_tolerance to determine whether the robot has satisfied the inversion or rotation constraint. The robot must be within both position and yaw tolerance for the handler to unlock the next path segment.
impact: Smaller values enforce a tighter heading match at the constraint point; larger values accept a broader angular offset before advancing.

description: Minimum heading change (in radians) between consecutive path poses that triggers a mandatory in-place rotation constraint when enforce_path_rotation is true. Defaults to 0.785 rad (~45 deg). Automatically reset to 0 when enforce_path_rotation is disabled.
impact: Lower values cause more frequent in-place rotations on gently curved paths; higher values only enforce rotations at sharp corners. Tune alongside enforce_path_rotation for your maneuverability requirements.

description: Number of path-point indices ahead of the robot's furthest reached path point that PathAlignCritic uses as its reference pose. Increasing this causes the critic to align trajectories to a point further along the path rather than the immediately closest one.
impact: Larger values make the robot aim further ahead on the path, smoothing out minor local deviations; too large a value may cause the critic to ignore upcoming sharp turns. Tune in proportion to the MPPI time horizon and desired look-ahead distance.

description: Maximum fraction of path points (from the start up to the furthest reached point) that may be in collision before PathAlignCritic is entirely suppressed. When dynamic obstacles block more than this ratio of the local path the critic skips scoring so the obstacle critic can handle avoidance.
impact: Lower values disable path-alignment scoring sooner in the presence of obstacles, giving more freedom for avoidance but potentially causing the robot to stray from the path. Higher values keep alignment active longer, which may force trajectories through obstacle-dense regions.

description: Stride used when iterating over trajectory time steps inside PathAlignCritic. Only every N-th trajectory point is compared against the reference path, reducing computational cost.
impact: Smaller step sizes give a denser and more accurate alignment cost at the expense of CPU time. Larger steps speed up scoring but may miss short-lived deviations from the path.

description: When true, PathAlignCritic includes heading error (yaw difference between each trajectory pose and its nearest path pose) in addition to lateral position error when computing alignment cost. When false only positional (XY) distance is used.
impact: Enabling this creates stronger pressure to match path headings throughout the maneuver, which helps on paths with rapid direction changes but can over-constrain the controller on smooth paths or when the path orientations are not well-defined.

description: Angular threshold used by PathAngleCritic. If the robot's heading error to the look-ahead path point is below this value the critic skips scoring, avoiding unnecessary corrections when the robot is already roughly aligned. Defaults to π/4 (~45 deg).
impact: Smaller values cause the critic to engage and penalise trajectories more frequently; larger values leave heading corrections to other critics unless the robot is significantly misaligned. A value of 0 would engage the critic at all times.

description: Selects how PathAngleCritic evaluates heading alignment. 0 = FORWARD_PREFERENCE: penalises trajectories that face away from the look-ahead point, favouring forward motion. 1 = NO_DIRECTIONAL_PREFERENCE: applies the same angular penalty regardless of forward/reverse direction, useful for omnidirectional robots. 2 = CONSIDER_FEASIBLE_PATH_ORIENTATIONS: uses the path's own yaw at the look-ahead point to choose the preferred direction, best for paths with explicit orientation constraints.
impact: Mode 0 is the safest default for differential-drive robots. Mode 1 enables symmetric reversing behaviour; it is automatically upgraded to mode 0 if vx_min >= 0. Mode 2 is most appropriate when the global planner provides reliable orientation information along the path.

description: Remaining path-length threshold below which GoalAngleCritic activates. The critic only penalises heading error when the robot is within this distance of the goal so that angular alignment is enforced only in the final approach phase.
impact: Larger values engage goal-angle scoring earlier, which helps the robot orient toward the goal sooner but may conflict with path-following critics further from the goal. Smaller values delay the alignment push until the robot is very close to the goal.

description: Rate at which the docking control loop runs. Controls how frequently velocity commands are computed and sent during dock approach, rotation-to-dock, and undock phases. Must be greater than 0.
impact: Higher frequencies yield smoother, more responsive approach trajectories but increase CPU load. Very low frequencies can cause sluggish or oscillatory behaviour near the dock. 50 Hz is suitable for most robot/dock combinations.

description: Maximum time allowed for the dock plugin to produce a first valid refined pose estimate after the robot arrives at the staging pose. If no detection arrives within this window, a FailedToDetectDock exception is raised and the action fails (or retries if max_retries allows).
impact: Increase if the external detector (camera, AR tag, etc.) needs extra warm-up time. Decrease to fail fast on unreliable perception rather than waiting at the staging pose.

description: How long the server waits for charging to begin after the robot makes physical contact with the dock (only applies to charging docks where isCharger() returns true). If charging is not detected within this window, a FailedToCharge exception is raised.
impact: Set slightly longer than the actual charger handshake delay of your hardware. Too short causes spurious failures; too long delays error recovery when contact is poor.

description: Maximum time allowed for the robot to complete the final approach to the dock (and for the reset-approach-to-staging phase on retries). If the timeout expires, a FailedToControl exception is raised.
impact: Should be generous enough to cover the full approach distance at minimum speed. Reduce if you want faster failure detection; increase for slow robots or long approach distances.

description: Linear distance tolerance for declaring that the robot has reached the staging pose during undocking. The robot must be within both undock_linear_tolerance and undock_angular_tolerance of the staging pose before undocking is considered complete.
impact: Smaller values require a more precise stop at the staging pose. Larger values allow undocking to complete sooner but leave the robot farther from the intended staging position.

description: Angular (yaw) tolerance for declaring that the robot has reached the staging pose during undocking. Used together with undock_linear_tolerance; both conditions must be satisfied.
impact: Smaller values enforce a tighter heading match at the staging pose after undocking. Larger values allow completion with a broader angular offset.

description: Maximum number of docking retry attempts before the action server reports failure. Each retry resets the robot to the staging pose and attempts the approach again. A DockingException during approach increments the retry counter.
impact: Higher values make docking more persistent at the cost of longer total action time on difficult docks. Set to 0 to fail immediately on the first error. 3 is a good starting point for robust field deployments.

description: Name of the robot's base TF frame, used to look up the current robot pose and to transform dock target poses into the base frame for velocity computation.
impact: Must match the actual base link frame published by your robot URDF / TF tree. Mismatches cause TF lookup failures and prevent docking from functioning.

description: Name of the fixed TF frame used as the reference frame for dock poses and collision checking during docking and undocking. Typically 'odom' or 'map'.
impact: Must match the frame in which dock poses are stored and in which the robot's odometry is expressed. Using 'map' provides globally-consistent docking if a map is available; 'odom' is simpler but drifts with odometric error over long distances.

description: Euclidean distance tolerance within which the robot is considered to have reached the pre-staging pose before the final dock approach begins. If the robot starts the action already within this radius of the staging pose, it skips directly to the approach phase.
impact: Increase to allow the docking action to begin the approach from a wider area around the staging pose; decrease for stricter pre-positioning. Setting too large can cause the robot to skip the staging phase and approach from an unexpected angle.

description: Proportional gain of the smooth-control-law that acts on the angle from the robot to the target pose (phi error). Part of the graceful/smooth control law used by the docking controller to compute curvature commands during dock approach.
impact: Higher values turn the robot toward the dock target more aggressively. Too high causes oscillation or overshoot; too low causes sluggish lateral convergence. Tune together with k_delta for stable approach trajectories.

description: Proportional gain of the smooth-control-law that acts on the heading error at the goal (delta error). Part of the graceful/smooth control law used by the docking controller; governs how strongly the robot corrects its heading as it nears the dock.
impact: Increasing this gain makes the robot orient toward the dock heading earlier and more forcefully. Excessively high values cause oscillatory heading correction; too low results in the robot arriving at the dock with the wrong orientation.

description: Minimum linear speed commanded during the docking approach. The smooth control law will not issue a forward/backward velocity below this value, ensuring the robot continues to move even when the computed command would otherwise stall.
impact: Set low enough that the robot can make fine positional corrections near the dock, but high enough to prevent stalling. Too high prevents the robot from creeping slowly into the dock; too low may cause jitter at the goal.

description: Maximum linear speed commanded during the docking approach. The smooth control law caps forward/backward velocity to this value regardless of the distance to the dock.
impact: Higher values speed up the approach from the staging pose but reduce accuracy near the dock and increase collision risk. Keep at or below the robot's comfortable low-speed driving limit; typical values are 0.1–0.5 m/s for indoor docking.

description: Maximum angular velocity commanded during the docking approach by the smooth control law. Angular commands computed from the k_phi/k_delta gains are clamped to ±v_angular_max before being sent to the robot.
impact: Lower values produce gentler turns during approach, which helps with precise alignment but slows lateral correction. Higher values allow sharper turns but may destabilise the approach if the robot has a slow odometry update rate.

description: Distance from the dock pose below which collision checking is suspended during the approach (docking) phase. Collision checking is also suspended for the region near the robot origin during undocking, to avoid false positives caused by the dock structure itself being inside the costmap.
impact: Increase if the dock structure or charging contacts appear in the costmap and falsely abort the final approach. Decrease if you want collision detection right up to the dock contact point. Setting too high disables collision checking for a large portion of the approach.

description: Maximum time allowed for the initial rotation to face the dock before the docking action aborts.
impact: Increase if the robot needs more time to rotate on approach. Too small a value will cause spurious failures on slow-rotating platforms.

description: Angular error threshold below which the rotate-to-dock phase is considered complete.
impact: Smaller values require more precise alignment before proceeding to the approach phase, improving dock alignment accuracy at the cost of additional time.

description: Topic name for the odometry messages used to estimate the robot's current velocity during docking approach.
impact: Must match the odometry source being published. Incorrect topic results in the server using zero velocity estimates, disabling stall detection.

description: Duration of the rolling window of odometry messages retained for velocity estimation during docking.
impact: Longer windows smooth out noisy odometry but add latency. Shorter windows are more responsive but noisier. Adjust if stall detection is triggering spuriously.

description: Whether to enable forward collision checking along the projected docking trajectory before executing each velocity command.
impact: Enabling prevents the robot from driving into obstacles during the docking approach but requires a valid costmap. Disable only in fully controlled environments to reduce CPU load.

description: Time horizon over which the docking trajectory is projected when checking for collisions.
impact: Longer horizons catch obstacles farther along the path but increase computation. Reduce if the collision checker is too conservative and rejects valid docking approaches.

description: Discrete time step used when simulating the docking trajectory for collision checking.
impact: Smaller steps improve collision detection resolution at the cost of more costmap lookups per cycle. Larger steps reduce CPU load but may miss narrow obstacles between sampled poses.

description: Ordered list of named dock-plugin instances to load at startup. Each name becomes the key under which the plugin is referenced in the dock database and by docking action goals. At least one entry is required — the server will refuse to configure if this list is missing or empty. Each listed name must also have a corresponding '<name>.plugin' type parameter.
impact: Must contain exactly the names used in the dock database (docks parameter or dock_database file). Mismatch between list entries and dock records causes a fatal startup error.

description: When true, the SimpleChargingDock plugin subscribes to the 'battery_state' topic (sensor_msgs/BatteryState) and reports isCharging() only when the measured current exceeds charging_threshold. When false, the dock simply reports isCharging() as true whenever the robot is within the docking pose threshold, without requiring a battery message.
impact: Set to true for robots that publish a BatteryState topic so charging is confirmed by electrical feedback. Set to false for docks without battery telemetry or during simulation, where pose proximity alone is used to declare successful docking.

description: When true, the SimpleChargingDock plugin subscribes to the 'detected_dock_pose' topic (geometry_msgs/PoseStamped) to obtain a refined dock pose from an external detector (e.g. a fiducial or AR-tag detector). The pose is filtered with an exponential moving average before being used to guide the final approach. When false, the dock pose is taken directly from the dock database without refinement.
impact: Enable for higher docking accuracy when an external perception pipeline is available. Disable in environments where no external detector is present; leaving it enabled without an active publisher causes the dock to stall waiting for external_detection_timeout.

description: Maximum age of the most recent external dock-pose detection before the pose is considered stale. Used by the PoseFilter to reject old detections. Only applies when use_external_detection_pose is true.
impact: Decrease to force the controller to react to fresh detections only; increase if the external detector runs slowly or has intermittent output. If no fresh detection arrives within this window, the last valid filtered pose is held, which can cause drift if the dock is moving.

description: X-axis offset applied to the raw external detection pose to account for the offset between the detected feature (e.g. AR tag) and the true dock contact point. Only applies when use_external_detection_pose is true. Combined with external_detection_translation_y and the rotation offsets to form the full correction transform.
impact: Tune this to the physical offset between your fiducial marker and the charging contacts on the dock. An incorrect value causes the robot to stop short of or overshoot the dock by the error amount.

description: Y-axis offset applied to the raw external detection pose to correct for lateral displacement between the detected feature and the actual dock contact point. Only applies when use_external_detection_pose is true.
impact: Set to the lateral distance between your fiducial marker and the dock's true alignment axis. An incorrect value causes the robot to approach with a lateral offset, risking a missed connection or mechanical damage.

description: Maximum Euclidean distance between the robot base and the dock pose below which the robot is considered docked. Only used when use_stall_detection is false. The docking action declares success once the robot is within this threshold and charging is confirmed (or battery checking is disabled).
impact: Smaller values require the robot to align more precisely before declaring success — good for tight connector tolerances but risks never triggering on imperfect floors. Larger values accept a looser stop, which may mean the connector does not fully engage.

description: Exponent applied to the ConstraintCritic cost before it is added to the total trajectory score. When set to 1 the penalty scales linearly with the magnitude of the velocity constraint violation; values greater than 1 apply a super-linear (power-law) penalty that more aggressively discourages trajectories that exceed vx_max, vx_min, or vy_max.
impact: Increase to more harshly penalise constraint-violating trajectories so the optimizer avoids them even when their path score is otherwise good. In practice 1 (linear) is sufficient for most robots; raising it above 2 may cause numerical instability in the MPPI cost.

description: Scalar weight multiplied into the ConstraintCritic penalty before accumulation. The critic penalises sampled trajectories whose velocities exceed the kinematic limits (vx_max, vx_min, vy_max for omni, minimum turning radius for Ackermann). This weight controls how strongly that violation discourages those trajectories relative to other critics.
impact: Higher values cause the MPPI optimizer to reject kinematically infeasible trajectories more strongly. Too low and the controller may select commands that transiently exceed hardware limits. Too high relative to path-following critics may make the robot overly cautious and slow.

description: Number of path points ahead of the furthest-reached point to use as the look-ahead target for the PathFollowCritic. Instead of scoring trajectories against the point the robot has already progressed to, the critic targets a point this many steps further along the path, providing forward progress incentive.
impact: Larger values encourage the robot to look further ahead and make bolder progress along the path. Smaller values keep the look-ahead closer to the current position, which can be safer in tight spaces but may cause the robot to slow prematurely. A value too large may exceed the end of the path and use the goal as the target.

description: Remaining local path length below which the PreferForwardCritic stops applying its forward-preference penalty. When the robot is within this distance of the goal, the critic deactivates so that reversing manoeuvres are not penalised during final goal-approach or docking.
impact: Increase to deactivate the forward-preference earlier, giving other critics (GoalAngleCritic, GoalCritic) more freedom during the approach phase. Decrease to continue penalising reverse trajectories until very close to the goal. Set higher if the robot needs to reverse to dock or reach tight goal orientations.

description: Stride used when publishing candidate trajectories for visualisation. Only every Nth sampled trajectory is published to the ~/candidate_trajectories MarkerArray topic. For example, the default of 5 publishes every 5th trajectory out of the full batch sampled by the MPPI optimizer.
impact: Smaller values publish more trajectories, giving a richer visualisation at the cost of higher bandwidth and RViz load. Larger values reduce the published set, lowering overhead. This parameter has no effect on controller behaviour — it is purely a visualisation aid.

description: Stride used when sampling poses along each published trajectory. Only every Nth time step within each visualised trajectory is drawn as a marker point, controlling the density of points along the trajectory line strip published to ~/candidate_trajectories.
impact: Smaller values draw more points per trajectory, producing smoother visualised arcs at the cost of more RViz markers. Larger values produce sparser lines. Like trajectory_step, this has no effect on actual controller behaviour.

description: When true, the DefaultOptimalTrajectoryValidator checks the full robot footprint (polygon) for collisions along the optimal trajectory before accepting it. When false only the robot's inscribed-circle centre point is checked, which is faster but may miss collisions in narrow corridors where the footprint protrudes.
impact: Enable for robots with non-circular footprints operating in tight spaces. Disabling (default) reduces per-cycle computation. When enabled the validator may reject an otherwise optimal trajectory and trigger the MPPI fallback loop, so ensure the costmap inflation radius is tuned appropriately to avoid over-rejection.

description: Ordered list of MPPI critic plugin names that are loaded and evaluated every control cycle. Each name must match a critic class registered via pluginlib (e.g. "ConstraintCritic", "CostCritic", "GoalCritic"). The nav2_bringup default uses [ConstraintCritic, CostCritic, GoalCritic, GoalAngleCritic, PathAlignCritic, PathFollowCritic, PathAngleCritic, PreferForwardCritic].
impact: Static — requires node restart to change. Removing critics reduces compute but loses the corresponding cost contribution. Adding or reordering critics changes how trajectories are ranked; each active critic adds a cost term whose relative influence is controlled by its cost_weight.

description: When true, the CostCritic checks the full robot footprint polygon for collisions at each trajectory point instead of the inscribed-circle centre. Must be consistent with the costmap's use_radius setting; MPPI raises a warning (or exception) if they are mismatched.
impact: Enable for non-circular robots in tight spaces to catch footprint-corner collisions the circle approximation misses. Disabling (default) is faster because only a single point lookup is needed per trajectory step. Requires the inflation layer radius to be at least half the robot's largest cross-section.

description: Exponent applied to the GoalCritic cost term before accumulating into the trajectory score. A value of 1 applies the cost linearly; 2 squares it, sharpening the gradient near the goal.
impact: Increase to 2 to make the optimizer much more aggressive about reaching the goal position as the robot approaches, at the cost of potentially overshooting or oscillating. Keep at 1 for smooth, predictable goal approach.

description: Scalar weight for the GoalCritic cost term, which penalises mean distance of sampled trajectories from the goal position. Only active when the robot is within threshold_to_consider of the goal.
impact: Higher values pull the robot more strongly towards the goal position during the final approach. Balance against GoalAngleCritic to achieve both precise position and correct heading at the goal.

description: Number of path indices ahead of the furthest-reached path point used as the look-ahead target for computing the angular error. The PathAngleCritic penalises trajectories whose final heading deviates from the direction of this look-ahead point.
impact: Larger values look further ahead on the path, encouraging the robot to pre-rotate earlier before curves. Smaller values react to the immediately upcoming path direction, which can cause oscillation on sharply curved paths.

description: Remaining local path length below which the PathAlignCritic is deactivated. When the robot is closer than this distance to the end of the local path segment, path-alignment scoring is suppressed so the GoalCritic can take full control of the final approach.
impact: Increase if the robot oscillates trying to align to the path while simultaneously approaching the goal. Decrease to keep path alignment active closer to the goal, which improves heading accuracy at the cost of potential oscillation.

description: Maximum remaining path length at which the SimpleGoalChecker will declare the goal reached. When the local path segment is shorter than this threshold and xy_goal_tolerance / yaw_goal_tolerance are also satisfied, the robot is considered to have achieved the goal.
impact: Increase to allow the goal checker to succeed while the robot is still further from the raw goal pose (useful when the planner produces a path that undershoots). Decrease towards 0 to require the robot to physically reach the goal coordinates before declaring success.

description: When true, the planner server will return the partial path it has computed so far if planning fails partway through a ComputePathThroughPoses request (e.g. one via-point is unreachable). When false, any planning failure raises an exception and the whole request fails.
impact: Enable on multi-goal missions where reaching all via-points is desirable but not strictly required. Disabled by default to preserve strict failure semantics; enabling it may cause the robot to stop short of a pose without error when one intermediate segment cannot be planned.

description: Maximum time (seconds) the planner server will wait for the global costmap to update before executing a planning request. Declared in nav2_planner/src/parameter_handler.cpp alongside the similar controller_server parameter (which defaults to 0.3 s).
impact: Increase if the global costmap is slow to update and the planner occasionally fails because it starts with a stale map. Setting it too high stalls planning on every request when the costmap is not actively updating; the controller_server equivalent should be tuned separately.

description: Controls ROS 2 service-call introspection for the planner server's action interfaces. 'disabled' turns introspection off; 'metadata' records request/response metadata only; 'contents' records full request and response data. Declared via nav2_ros_common/include/nav2_ros_common/node_utils.hpp and requires rclcpp >= 29.
impact: Enabling introspection is useful for debugging planning request flow but adds publishing overhead. Use 'metadata' for lightweight tracing; 'contents' only for focused debugging sessions. Keep 'disabled' in production.

description: When true, the MPPI controller publishes sampled trajectory markers and the optimal trajectory on visualization topics. Declared in nav2_mppi_controller/src/controller.cpp via the MPPI ParametersHandler (dynamic by default).
impact: Enable during development to inspect sampled trajectories in RViz. Adds non-trivial publishing overhead — disable in production or resource-constrained deployments. The nav2_bringup example YAML sets this to true.

description: Remaining local path length below which the PathFollowCritic stops scoring trajectories. When the robot is within this distance of the end of the local path the critic deactivates, allowing other critics (GoalCritic, GoalAngleCritic) to dominate the final approach. Declared in nav2_mppi_controller/src/critics/path_follow_critic.cpp.
impact: Increase to hand off control to goal-approach critics earlier, which can smooth the final docking manoeuvre. Decrease to keep forward path-following active until very close to the goal — useful when the path ends well before the physical goal and premature hand-off causes drift.

description: Enables or disables the ConstraintCritic in the MPPI optimizer. The ConstraintCritic penalises sampled trajectories whose velocities violate the kinematic limits (vx_max, vx_min, vy_max, or minimum turning radius). The 'enabled' field is common to all MPPI critics and is declared in nav2_mppi_controller/include/nav2_mppi_controller/critic_function.hpp.
impact: Disabling removes kinematic-limit enforcement from the cost function. Only disable when another mechanism prevents out-of-limit commands, or during debugging. Leaving it enabled ensures the MPPI optimizer avoids trajectories that the motion model cannot execute.

description: Direction in which the SimpleChargingDock plugin approaches the dock. "forward" drives the robot forward into the dock; "backward" reverses the robot onto the dock. Must match the physical orientation of the charging connector relative to the robot base.
impact: Using "backward" enables reverse-approach docking and may be combined with rotate_to_dock to spin the robot 180° before backing in. An incorrect setting will drive the robot in the wrong direction and fail to make contact with the dock.

description: Yaw component of a fixed RPY rotation applied to the externally detected dock pose before it is used by the controller. This corrects for a mismatch between the detector frame and the expected dock approach frame.
impact: Set to the rotation offset (in radians) that aligns the detector output with the dock approach direction. An incorrect value will cause the robot to approach the dock from the wrong angle.

description: Pitch component of a fixed RPY rotation applied to the externally detected dock pose. Together with roll and yaw, this transforms the detector output into the dock approach frame expected by the controller.
impact: Typically set to π/2 (1.57) to convert a pose from a detector that reports orientation in camera frame to the robot navigation frame. Changing this incorrectly will misalign the dock approach and cause docking failures.

description: Roll component of a fixed RPY rotation applied to the externally detected dock pose. Together with pitch and yaw, this corrects the detector frame orientation so the dock pose is expressed in the robot navigation frame.
impact: Typically set to -π/2 (-1.57) for camera-based detectors whose Z-axis points forward. An incorrect value rotates the dock pose about the wrong axis and will misalign the approach trajectory.

description: Low-pass filter coefficient for the detected dock pose. At each step the filtered pose is updated as: filt = (1 - coef) * filt + coef * measurement. A value of 0.0 disables filtering (raw measurements passed through); a value of 1.0 removes all smoothing.
impact: Lower values produce a heavily smoothed dock pose that reduces jitter from a noisy detector, but introduces lag. Higher values track rapid dock motion faster at the cost of more noise in the approach trajectory. Tune based on detector noise level and approach speed.

description: Offset along the dock pose X-axis used to compute the staging pose from which fine docking control begins. A negative value places the staging pose in front of the dock (the robot stops short and then uses the controller to finish the approach).
impact: Larger magnitude (more negative) gives the controller more room to align before contact, improving accuracy on imperfect approaches. Too small a magnitude and the robot may not have enough room to converge to the dock pose before timing out.

description: When true, the SimpleChargingDock plugin subscribes to joint_states and uses motor stall (low velocity, high effort on the configured joints) to detect dock contact instead of relying on pose proximity (docking_threshold). Requires stall_joint_names, stall_velocity_threshold, and stall_effort_threshold to also be set.
impact: Enable for robots whose drive joints stall when physically pressed against the dock connector, providing a reliable mechanical contact signal. When disabled, dock contact is inferred from pose proximity alone, which is less reliable on slippery floors or with imprecise localization.

description: ROS topic providing the raw costmap used by the docking controller to check for collisions along the projected approach trajectory. Declared as controller.costmap_topic in the source. Only active when use_collision_detection is true.
impact: Must point to a live costmap that covers the docking approach area. An incorrect or stale topic will cause false collision detections (blocking docking) or missed obstacles. Match the local costmap namespace configured in your Nav2 stack.

description: Period of the main simulator update timer. Controls how frequently the simulator integrates cmd_vel into the robot pose and re-broadcasts TF transforms.
impact: Smaller values give finer motion integration and smoother TF output but increase CPU usage. Values larger than the controller frequency will cause the simulated robot to appear to jump between poses.

description: TF frame ID used as the fixed map frame. The simulator publishes the map→odom transform with this as the parent frame.
impact: Must match the map frame used by all other Nav2 nodes. A mismatch causes TF lookup failures and navigation breakdowns.

description: TF frame ID attached to the simulated laser scanner. Published LaserScan messages use this as their header frame_id.
impact: Must match the laser sensor frame in the robot URDF so that costmap obstacle layers can project scan hits correctly.

description: Minimum valid range for the simulated laser scan. Readings closer than this distance are discarded.
impact: Set to match the real sensor's minimum range. Too large a value creates a blind spot around the robot that can cause missed close obstacles.

description: Maximum detection range of the simulated laser scan. Rays that do not hit any obstacle within this distance report infinity (or range_max − 0.1 when scan_use_inf is false).
impact: Larger values allow obstacle detection further ahead but increase ray-casting compute time. Match to the real sensor's specified maximum range.

description: Start angle of the simulated laser scan arc, measured counter-clockwise from the scanner's forward direction.
impact: Together with scan_angle_max, defines the angular field of view. Narrowing the arc reduces CPU load but creates blind sectors; match to the real sensor spec.

description: End angle of the simulated laser scan arc, measured counter-clockwise from the scanner's forward direction.
impact: Together with scan_angle_min, defines the angular field of view. Must be greater than scan_angle_min; match to the real sensor spec.

description: Angular resolution between consecutive laser scan rays. Determines the number of range samples as (scan_angle_max − scan_angle_min) / scan_angle_increment.
impact: Smaller values produce denser scans with finer obstacle resolution but increase ray-casting and costmap update CPU cost. Match to the real sensor angular resolution.

description: When true, rays that reach scan_range_max without hitting an obstacle are reported as infinity. When false, they are reported as scan_range_max − 0.1 m.
impact: Most Nav2 costmap layers treat infinity as a free-space indicator and ignore the ray. Setting false encodes max-range hits as finite values, which some older sensor layers interpret as obstacles — keep true unless your pipeline requires finite no-hit ranges.
